days.title: FSM Foundations and Safety Leadership
days.focus: Vocabulary, lifecycle, timing, safety case, culture and confirmation measures.
days.deliverable: One-page lifecycle map plus a fault-to-safety-evidence trace.

days.title: Item Definition, HARA and Functional Safety Concept
days.focus: Brake/ESC item boundary, hazardous events, ASIL, safety goals and degraded operation.
days.deliverable: Mini HARA and FSC for unintended braking.

days.title: SysML, MBSE and System Safety Architecture
days.focus: Requirements, interfaces, behavior, TSC, HSI, independence and architecture review.
days.deliverable: Logical ESC architecture with safety interfaces and modes.

days.title: Requirements, Traceability, Suppliers and Configuration
days.focus: DOORS/Polarion, DIA, SEooC, reuse, change and configuration management.

days.title: FMEA, FTA, DFA and ASIL Decomposition
days.focus: Safety-oriented analyses and their feedback into architecture.
days.deliverable: Mini FMEA + FTA + DFA for one ESC fault path.

days.title: Hardware Safety and Random Hardware Failures
days.focus: HSRs, fault classes, SPFM/LFM, PMHF, sensors, MCU and actuator safety.
days.deliverable: Hardware safety concept and diagnostic evidence plan.

days.title: Software Safety Architecture and Verification
days.focus: SSRs, FFI, monitoring, unit/integration verification, MBD and tool confidence.

days.title: Brakes, ESC and Vehicle-Control Safety
days.focus: Vehicle dynamics, control chain, plausibility, unintended intervention and degradation.
days.deliverable: End-to-end ESC intervention and fault-response model.

days.title: V&V, CAN, OBD, Production and Service
days.focus: Test strategy, fault injection, network safety, diagnostics, validation and post-development.

days.title: Senior Interview, Safety Case and Release Decisions
days.focus: Leadership scenarios, assessments, architecture trade-offs and end-to-end mock interview.

tracks.name: FSM
tracks.desc: Lifecycle, planning, governance, safety case, confirmation, competence and release.

tracks.name: HARA
tracks.desc: Item, hazards, S/E/C, ASIL, safety goals and FSC.

tracks.name: SysML
tracks.desc: Requirements, structure, behavior, interfaces, allocation and traceability.

tracks.name: System Architecture
tracks.desc: TSC, HSI, mechanisms, FTTI, independence and degraded states.

tracks.name: Brakes
tracks.desc: Base braking, brake assist, ABS, brake-by-wire and actuation safety.

tracks.name: ESC
tracks.desc: Vehicle dynamics, yaw control, sensing, arbitration and degradation.

tracks.name: Control Functions
tracks.desc: Estimation, control chain, monitors, timing, limits and arbitration.

tracks.name: Requirements
tracks.desc: Quality, hierarchy, allocation, traceability, variants and change.

tracks.name: Safety Analysis
tracks.desc: FMEA, FTA, assumptions, fault classification and design feedback.

tracks.name: DFA
tracks.desc: Common causes, cascades, coupling factors and independence.

tracks.name: Hardware
tracks.desc: HSRs, SPFM/LFM, PMHF/EEC, sensors, MCU and actuators.

tracks.name: Software
tracks.desc: SSRs, architecture, FFI, implementation and verification.

tracks.name: Verification
tracks.desc: Reviews, analyses, integration, fault injection and evidence.

tracks.name: CAN
tracks.desc: Contracts, E2E, freshness, timing, overload and bus faults.

tracks.name: OBD
tracks.desc: Service diagnostics versus time-critical safety diagnostics.

tracks.name: Supplier
tracks.desc: DIA, distributed development, SEooC/AoU and legacy evidence.

matrix: FSM
Safety lifecycle, roles, planning, anomalies, safety case and release
Part 2 Clauses 5–7; Part 8; Part 7
Connect governance decisions to technical evidence and residual risk.
SysML / MBSE
Model item, requirements, architecture, behavior, interfaces and evidence links
Part 3 item/FSC; Part 4 TSC; Part 8 requirements; Part 9 analyses
Use models to make safety intent reviewable, not as decoration.
System Architecture
TSC, mechanisms, HSI, FTTI, independence and degradation
Part 4 Clause 6; Part 9 Clauses 5–8
Explain nominal behavior and failure behavior together.
Brakes
Unintended, lost and asymmetric braking; actuation; availability
Parts 3–9; Part 7 for production/service
Domain dynamics determine hazards, timing, safe states and validation limits.
ESC / Control
State estimation, yaw control, arbitration, monitoring and degradation
Parts 3, 4, 6 and 9
Review vehicle dynamics and safety architecture as one end-to-end loop.
FMEA / FTA / DFA
Failure modes, top events, combinations, common causes and design action
Part 9 Clauses 7–8; Parts 5 and 6
Analysis must feed design and tests, not remain a report.
Hardware Safety
Fault class, coverage, SPFM/LFM, PMHF, sensors, MCU and drivers
Part 5 Clauses 6–10; Part 11
Vendor evidence requires item-specific integration and AoU verification.
Software Safety
SSRs, FFI, monitoring, unit design, integration and tools
Part 6; Part 8 Clause 11
Address temporal and communication interference, not memory only.
Requirements / Tools
Typed traceability, baselines, change, variants and test evidence
Part 8 Clauses 6–10
Enable semantic impact analysis and configuration-correct evidence.
CAN / OBD
Signal integrity, freshness, bus failures, DTCs and service behavior
Parts 4, 6, 7 and 8
Frame CRC and DTC storage do not replace application safety reaction.
V&V
MIL/SIL/HIL, fault injection, rigs, vehicle and safety validation
Parts 4–6; Part 8 Clause 9
Choose test level by observability, fidelity and physical effect.
Supplier / SEooC
DIA, assumptions, safety manual, legacy and integration evidence
Part 8 Clause 5 and 12–16; Parts 10/11
Certificates never remove integrator responsibility.
jdMap: TSC, interfaces, mechanisms, trade-offs and brake/ESC case
Requirement quality, SysML links, DOORS/Polarion and V&V evidence
BDD/IBD, states, sequences, allocation and governance
HARA, FMEA, FTA, DFA and decomposition
Baselines, variants, impact and evidence invalidation
E2E/freshness, bus faults, DTC and service interaction
Culture, supplier escalation, assessment and release

questions.question: What is automotive functional safety, and how does it differ from reliability, quality, cybersecurity, and SOTIF?
answer: Functional safety addresses unreasonable risk caused by malfunctioning behavior of safety-related E/E systems.
Reliability concerns continued intended operation; safe degradation can reduce reliability while preserving safety.
Quality broadly prevents defects; ISO 26262 adds risk-based integrity, safety requirements, independence, analyses and evidence.
Cybersecurity treats malicious actions; SOTIF treats hazards from functional insufficiency or foreseeable misuse without a fault.
probes: Give one braking example for each domain.
Can a shutdown be safe but unreliable?
How can a cyber event enter a safety case?

questions.question: Walk through the automotive safety lifecycle and name the principal evidence from concept to decommissioning.
answer: Item definition and HARA create hazardous events, safety goals and the FSC.
The TSC and system architecture refine and allocate technical requirements to hardware and software.
Unit/component/system/vehicle integration verifies the implementation; safety validation evaluates achievement of safety goals.
Requirements, configuration, change, verification, documentation, tools, production, service and decommissioning support the lifecycle.
A senior answer shows bidirectional traceability and progressive safety-case construction.
probes: How does a field change re-enter the lifecycle?

questions.question: Differentiate item, system, element, component, hardware part and software unit using ESC.
answer: The item is the vehicle-level function or set of functions subject to the safety lifecycle and defined boundaries.
A system is a set of interacting elements; ESC can include sensing, computation, communication and actuation.
An element is any architectural building block; ECU, inertial sensor cluster and hydraulic modulator are examples.
Components, hardware parts and software units are successively lower implementation levels chosen for allocation, analysis and verification.
probes: Is the wheel-speed sensor inside the item?

questions.question: Explain hazard, hazardous event, malfunctioning behavior, safety goal, safety requirement and safety mechanism.
answer: A hazard is a potential source of harm; a hazardous event combines a hazard with an operational situation.
Malfunctioning behavior is item failure or unintended behavior that contributes to the event.
A safety goal is a top-level safety requirement derived from an ASIL-rated hazardous event.
Functional, technical, hardware and software safety requirements refine the goal.
A safety mechanism detects, controls or mitigates faults so a required safe or degraded state is reached in time.
probes: Classify unintended braking at highway speed.
Give one safety mechanism and requirement.

questions.question: Describe fault → error → failure and distinguish systematic faults from random hardware faults.
answer: A fault can create an internal erroneous state; an error can propagate to an externally observable failure.
Systematic faults are related deterministically to development, manufacturing or use conditions and are controlled by process and verification.
Random hardware faults arise unpredictably from physical hardware effects and are controlled by architecture, diagnostics and quantitative evaluation.
The relevant abstraction and propagation path must be explicit in the safety analysis.
probes: Give an ESC software systematic fault.
Give a random hardware propagation path.
Why is software not assigned a hardware-like FIT rate?

questions.question: For electronic braking, what can safe state, degraded state and emergency operation mean?
answer: The required state is hazard-specific; disabling everything is not automatically safe when availability matters.
A safe state may retain base braking while inhibiting automatic pressure requests and warning the driver.
A degraded state may preserve ABS or limited braking while disabling ESC and constraining torque.
Emergency operation is temporary safe-enough operation when an immediate safe state cannot be reached or maintained.
Specify transition, duration, driver interaction and assumptions.
probes: Can no ESC intervention itself be hazardous?

questions.question: Explain FTTI, detection time, reaction time and allocated fault-handling time using an ESC actuator fault.
answer: FTTI is the item-level interval from fault occurrence until a hazardous event can occur without safety action.
Detection plus reaction and physical settling must fit inside the allocated timing budget.
Include task periods, bus delays, debounce, actuator shutoff and hydraulic pressure decay.
Use worst-case phase, jitter and environmental conditions rather than average timing.
Verify allocation at element level and final behavior at system/vehicle level.
probes: How does bus congestion enter the budget?

questions.question: What is a safety case, and what makes it convincing?
answer: It is a structured argument supported by evidence that the released item achieves functional safety in its defined context.
It links goals to requirements, architecture, analyses, verification, validation, assumptions and process evidence.
Claims, context, assumptions, evidence confidence and residual risk must be explicit.
Evidence must match the exact configuration and be internally consistent.
A strong review challenges missing links, stale evidence and unresolved anomalies.
probes: Give a top-level ESC safety claim.
How do open anomalies affect release?
What evidence is invalidated by an architecture change?

questions.question: A project follows templates but suppresses safety concerns to protect schedule. Is it functionally safe?
answer: Artifacts alone do not establish safety; safety culture requires authority, competence and transparent anomaly escalation.
Document and assess the concern, protect independent judgment and escalate through governance.
Schedule pressure cannot justify weakened classifications or hidden evidence gaps.
Corrective action can include replanning, independent review, additional verification and release restrictions.
probes: How do you protect the engineer raising the concern?
refs: ISO 26262-2 safety culture and anomaly management

questions.question: Differentiate confirmation review, functional-safety audit and functional-safety assessment.
answer: A confirmation review examines whether selected safety work products meet their objectives.
An audit examines whether required processes are implemented and followed.
An assessment judges whether functional safety has been achieved for the item using process and product evidence.
Independence requirements increase with ASIL and confirmation-measure type.
Scope, competence, findings, closure and release authority must be distinguished.
probes: Can an author review their own work?
What should an ASIL-D assessment agenda cover?
How do supplier findings feed item assessment?

questions.question: Create an item definition boundary for ESC. What belongs inside, outside and at the interfaces?
answer: Define intended functions, variants, modes, dependencies and interactions.
Possible internal elements include control logic and modulator; interfaces include wheel speed, yaw, lateral acceleration, steering, pedal, torque and HMI.
State external systems and assumptions: power, CAN/gateway, base brakes, tires, service tools and driver.
Specify physical, functional and communication interfaces plus environmental constraints.
Choose boundaries for complete hazard analysis and clear ownership, not to minimize responsibility.
probes: Is the pedal inside the item?
How do variants alter the boundary?
What changes with a shared inertial sensor?

questions.question: Why should HARA begin with item-level malfunctioning behavior rather than a component FMEA list?
answer: HARA evaluates vehicle-level hazardous events without dependence on a chosen implementation.
Component-first thinking can miss functional interaction hazards and unintended behavior.
FMEA later analyzes how component faults violate safety requirements.
Technology-independent goals preserve architectural freedom.
probes: Name a hazard missed by sensor-only thinking.
How do conflicting brake requests appear?

questions.question: List representative hazardous events for unintended braking, loss of braking support and incorrect ESC intervention.
answer: Combine malfunctions with situations such as highway driving, cornering, low friction, close following, intersections, towing and service mode.
Examples include unexpected deceleration causing rear impact, insufficient deceleration at an intersection, differential braking causing yaw and loss of stability intervention on a slippery curve.
Retain situations only when severity, exposure or controllability differ materially.
Identify affected occupants and other road users.
probes: How does reversing change the analysis?
Does speed alone define the situation?
How would trailer operation be included?

questions.question: Justify Severity, Exposure and Controllability for unintended differential braking on a highway curve.
answer: Severity is based on potential harm, not fault probability.
Exposure is frequency or duration of the operational situation, not component failure rate.
Controllability considers expected avoidance by drivers or other road users, suddenness, dynamics and reaction time.
Record data, assumptions, boundary cases and conservative rationale.
Review consistency across similar hazards and variants.
probes: Why is exposure not sensor failure probability?

questions.question: Draft implementation-independent safety goals for unintended braking and loss of braking support.
answer: State the prohibited or required item behavior and assign the ASIL.
Constrain unintended deceleration/yaw or preserve required braking without naming a particular MCU or CRC.
Include operating states, availability, safe state and timing attributes where needed.
Trace every ASIL-rated event and justify combined goals.
probes: Can two hazards share one goal?
Should a goal mention CAN CRC?

questions.question: Derive functional safety requirements preventing hazardous unintended ESC yaw intervention.
answer: Define authorization and plausibility, independent monitoring, arbitration, inhibition, warning and degraded operation.
Specify functional behavior, timing, modes and interfaces without detailed implementation.
Allocate to sensing, control, actuator control, power and HMI.
Define safety-validation criteria and assumptions.
probes: What monitor can be sufficiently independent?
How are brake and ESC requests arbitrated?
What is the functional safe state?

questions.question: Why can a fail-silent concept be insufficient for braking and stability functions?
answer: Braking availability can itself be safety-related, especially in brake-by-wire.
The concept may require fault tolerance, redundancy, degraded modes or temporary emergency operation.
Analyze loss, unintended function and mode transitions.
Warnings cannot compensate for dynamics that become hazardous before reaction.
Specify retained subfunctions, limits, duration and exit conditions.
probes: Can base braking be an external measure?

questions.question: A team claims a dashboard warning lowers controllability because it appears within two seconds. Challenge it.
answer: Controllability depends on whether the user can perceive and act before harm, not on warning existence.
Fast unintended yaw or braking may occur before the warning is useful.
Assess latency, salience, workload, available maneuver and effects on following traffic.
Use human-factors or vehicle evidence for the specific scenario.
Treat warning effectiveness as an assumption to validate.
probes: What evidence would make the warning credible?
How do other road users affect C?

questions.question: A sport ESC mode allows larger sideslip before intervention. What safety work must be revisited?
answer: Perform impact analysis on item definition, modes, intended behavior and interfaces.
Revisit HARA because controllability and vehicle response change.
Update FSC/TSC, limits, diagnostics, warnings, requirements and analyses.
Reverify software, calibrations, mode transitions and affected variants.
Update safety case and controlled baselines.

questions.question: What weaknesses do you look for in a brake or ESC HARA?
answer: Implementation-biased hazards, missing situations and confusion of hazard with failure mode.
Weak exposure/controllability rationale and hidden reliance on warnings.
Design-prescriptive goals, unclear safe states or missing availability.
Omitted interaction with ADAS, powertrain, trailer, service, communication and degraded modes.
Poor completeness, consistency and assumption verification.
probes: How do you sample-check a large HARA?

questions.question: Which SysML views are useful for functional safety and what question does each answer?
answer: Requirement diagrams show hierarchy, derivation, allocation and verification.
BDD/IBD show decomposition, interfaces, flows, redundancy and shared resources.
Activity, sequence and state diagrams show behavior, timing, fault reaction and degraded transitions.
Parametrics can capture timing and engineering budgets.
The model is valuable only with controlled semantics, ownership and configuration.
probes: How is a safety mechanism represented?

questions.question: Model the trace from hazardous event to safety validation.
answer: Create artifacts for hazardous event, goal, FSR, TSR, HSR/SSR, architecture, test and evidence.
Use typed relations such as derive, satisfy and verify consistently.
Attach ASIL, timing and allocation attributes.
Enable bidirectional impact analysis and baseline the model with textual requirements.
Review semantic correctness, not just link count.
probes: Can one test verify several requirements?
How do you prevent circular traceability?

questions.question: Create a logical ESC architecture and identify safety-relevant interfaces.
answer: Use sensing, acquisition, state estimation, control, arbitration, actuation, diagnostics, power and HMI blocks.
Identify wheel speed, steering, yaw/lateral acceleration, pedal, torque, pressure and warning interfaces.
Specify units, range, rate, freshness, validity, initialization and ownership.
Show monitor boundaries, diverse paths and common resources.
Separate logical architecture from ECU deployment before mapping to physical elements.

questions.question: Use state and sequence models to describe normal, degraded, emergency and safe-state behavior.
answer: Define explicit modes, guards, events, timing and allowed functions.
Model detection, debounce, arbitration, warning, actuator response and recovery.
Use sequences for cross-element timing and state machines for persistent modes.
Include simultaneous faults, reset, startup, shutdown and communication loss.
Derive review and test scenarios from the model.
probes: What happens when two faults occur in transition?
How is a latched fault modeled?

questions.question: What is the difference between FSC and TSC?
answer: FSC specifies functional safety requirements and preliminary allocation to achieve goals.
TSC refines them into technical requirements, system architecture, safety mechanisms and hardware/software allocation.
TSC addresses systematic failures, random hardware faults, timing, interfaces, external measures and verification.
Traceability must preserve the original hazard-control intent.
probes: Give one FSR and TSR refinements.

questions.question: What belongs in the HSI for a safety-related brake ECU?
answer: Signal definitions, timing, ranges, scaling, validity, initialization and ownership.
Registers, memory, interrupts, watchdog, reset, clock, power modes and error reporting.
Safety-mechanism configuration and software responsibilities for detection, reaction and tests.
Bootloader, calibration, startup/shutdown and communication constraints.
Verification responsibilities and MCU safety-manual assumptions.
probes: What changes when the bootloader changes?

questions.question: Two redundant yaw channels use separate algorithms but the same supply and clock. Can independence or ASIL decomposition be claimed?
answer: Algorithmic separation alone does not prove independence.
Analyze common supply, clock, thermal, communication, calibration, specification and tool dependencies.
Use DFA to identify initiators and coupling factors and add containment or diversity.
Decomposition requires redundant safety requirements and sufficient independence.
Confirmation measures remain tied to the original safety-goal ASIL.
probes: What diverse source could be used?

questions.question: Explain freedom from interference at system and software levels.
answer: It prevents one element from causing cascading failure that violates another safety requirement.
System channels include power, clock, thermal, mechanical, communication and shared resources.
Software channels include memory, timing, execution, communication, peripherals and services.
Partitioning must be supported by configuration, analysis and fault tests.
Residual dependencies belong in DFA and the safety case.
probes: How can a QM logger affect ASIL-D control?

questions.question: A SysML model and DOORS database disagree on an interface timeout. Which is authoritative and what do you do?
answer: Authority must be defined in the project information/configuration model; ambiguity is a process defect.
Identify the controlled baseline and freeze affected decisions.
Analyze impact on design, timing, supplier obligations, analysis and tests.
Resolve through controlled change and synchronize derived artifacts.
Invalidate and regenerate affected evidence in the safety case.
probes: How do you manage model-generated requirements?

questions.question: What ten questions do you ask before approving an ESC TSC?
answer: Are goals and FSRs completely allocated and traced?
Are normal, degraded, emergency and safe states unambiguous?
Are FTTI and fault-handling budgets complete?
Are sensor, CAN, power and actuator interfaces specified?
Do mechanisms have clear detection, reaction and coverage?
Are independence, coexistence and shared resources analyzed?
Are HSI and HW/SW allocations consistent?
Are variants, calibration, reset and recovery covered?
Are verification and validation criteria feasible?
Are assumptions, supplier obligations and open risks controlled?
probes: What evidence is required at this review?

questions.question: What makes a safety requirement reviewable, implementable and verifiable?
answer: It is unique, atomic, unambiguous, feasible, necessary and consistent.
It states conditions, behavior, limits, timing, modes and fault response at the correct abstraction.
Attributes include ASIL, source, allocation, rationale, owner and verification method.
It has upward/downward traceability and measurable acceptance criteria.
Avoid vague terms such as quickly, robust and as appropriate.
probes: When is one requirement too large?
How do you specify prohibited behavior?

questions.question: Design a traceability chain for an unintended-braking safety goal.
answer: Trace event → goal → FSR → TSR → HSR/SSR → architecture → analysis → verification → validation.
Trace assumptions and external measures to owners and evidence.
Link changes, anomalies and variants to affected artifacts.
Use coverage reports to find gaps but review semantic correctness.
Preserve the exact released baseline in the safety case.

questions.question: Review: “The ECU shall detect all wheel-speed sensor failures.” What is wrong?
answer: “All failures” is undefined and unverifiable; enumerate relevant failure modes and conditions.
Define threshold, timing, debounce, status, coverage target and reaction.
Separate detection and system reaction where appropriate.
State assumptions such as minimum speed and availability of comparison signals.
Define analysis/tests for stuck, drift, intermittent, missing and correlated failures.
probes: What limitations exist at low speed?

questions.question: How would you structure DOORS or Polarion for safety requirements, tests, analyses and releases?
answer: Define artifact types, attributes, typed links, workflows, roles and review states.
Maintain safety classification and end-to-end traceability.
Use baselines, suspect links, history, approvals and variant fields.
Link tests/results to exact hardware, software and calibration baselines.
Use dashboards for coverage and risk without replacing semantic review.
probes: How do you prevent duplicate sources of truth?

questions.question: A wheel-speed filter constant changes late in validation. Describe the safety response.
answer: Open a controlled change with motivation, owner, variants and baseline.
Assess control performance, fault detection, FTTI, HARA assumptions, requirements, analyses and tests.
Obtain safety approval before implementation and update traceability.
Run targeted and regression verification.
Release only a consistent software/calibration/evidence configuration.
probes: What if the plant changes the value?

questions.question: What should a Development Interface Agreement contain for an ESC ECU supplier?
answer: Allocate lifecycle activities, work products, safety requirements, ASILs, assumptions and interfaces.
Define responsibility for analyses, metrics, integration, validation and safety-case evidence.
Define change, anomaly, configuration, tool, competence and escalation processes.
Specify formats, milestones, review criteria and evidence access.
Cover production, service and third-party components.

questions.question: Explain SEooC and Assumptions of Use when integrating a safety MCU.
answer: An SEooC is developed for assumed functionality, environment, interfaces and safety requirements outside a specific item.
The integrator maps item requirements and verifies every relevant AoU.
Configure watchdog, ECC, clock, reset, fault reporting and periodic tests as required.
Resolve unmet AoU with external measures or redesign.
Use the exact MCU configuration and vendor safety information in item analyses.
probes: What if one AoU is unmet?

questions.question: A legacy brake component was not developed to ISO 26262. How can its use be justified?
answer: Perform impact analysis and determine applicable lifecycle tailoring.
Evaluate development evidence, field history, modifications, interfaces and use profile.
Consider hardware-element evaluation, software-component qualification, proven-in-use, external measures or redevelopment.
Address both systematic and random faults and record limitations.
Include the argument and verified assumptions in the safety case.

questions.question: An OEM allocates ASIL-D requirements but withholds goals and use assumptions. How do you proceed?
answer: Explain the need for context to interpret safe state, timing, interfaces and verification.
Use DIA discussions to obtain minimum safety context while protecting confidentiality.
Document explicit assumptions and limits; do not silently invent context.
Escalate unresolved ambiguity and constrain compliance claims.
Use an SEooC-style approach only with controlled assumptions later verified by the integrator.
probes: Can the supplier still do FMEA?

questions.question: What metrics govern safety requirements without encouraging superficial compliance?
answer: Coverage: traceability, allocation and verification completeness.
Quality: review findings, ambiguity defects, volatility and reopened items.
Flow: aging of changes/anomalies and supplier response.
Evidence: passed tests by ASIL and exact baseline plus invalidated evidence.
Use metrics as investigation signals and sample semantic quality.
probes: Which metric is easiest to game?
How do you show variant coverage?
What belongs on an executive dashboard?

questions.question: Compare FMEA and FTA. Why should a senior safety engineer use both?
answer: FMEA is bottom-up: element failure modes, causes, local/system effects, controls and actions.
FTA is top-down: a defined top event is decomposed into causal combinations.
FMEA supports systematic coverage; FTA exposes combinations and architectural paths.
They should be iterated with requirements and architecture and cross-checked.
Neither replaces DFA or quantitative hardware evaluation.
probes: How do you align abstraction levels?

questions.question: Perform a mini-FMEA for a wheel-speed signal used by ABS and ESC.
answer: Failure modes include missing, stuck, drift, noise, intermittent, wrong wheel, delayed and correlated wrong value.
Effects include false or missed intervention and wrong vehicle-speed or slip estimate.
Causes include sensor, tone ring, wiring, supply, acquisition, communication, mapping and conversion.
Controls include electrical checks, range/rate, cross-wheel/model plausibility, freshness and degraded control.
Record timing, coverage assumptions, safety requirements and verification actions.
probes: How do you detect all four sensors drifting?
What is safe at low speed?

questions.question: Build the first two levels of an FTA for hazardous unintended braking.
answer: Define the top event with operating state, magnitude and duration.
Branches can include erroneous request, bad arbitration, actuator applies pressure without request, failed inhibit and failed reaction.
Add input corruption, algorithm fault, communication corruption, power/reset transient, driver-stage and mechanical faults.
Represent combinations where a primary fault plus monitor failure is required.
Use results to refine design, DFA, fault injection and quantitative analysis.
probes: How is a stuck valve modeled?
What is a minimal cut set for a monitored request?

questions.question: Differentiate common-cause, cascading, common-mode failure and dependent-failure initiator.
answer: Dependent failures are not statistically independent.
A common cause affects multiple elements without one element failure causing the other.
A cascading failure propagates from one element to another through a coupling path.
Common mode describes similar failure behavior and can result from a common cause.
A DFI is the root cause; coupling can be physical, functional, specification, process or tool-related.
probes: Give a brake example for each.
Can common requirements create common cause?

questions.question: Perform a DFA thought exercise on two-channel steering-angle acquisition.
answer: List channels, safety mechanisms and independence assumptions.
Check shared mechanics, supply, ground, connector, ADC/reference, MCU, clock, communication, calibration and specification.
Identify initiators, coupling and effects on both channels and the comparator.
Use diversity, separated acquisition, independent vehicle-dynamics plausibility and containment.
Plan schematic/layout review, fault injection, environmental and integration evidence.

questions.question: Explain ASIL decomposition, prerequisites and common misuse.
answer: It apportions redundant safety requirements to sufficiently independent paths that jointly achieve the same goal.
Both paths must be truly redundant for the safety requirement and independence must be demonstrated.
The original safety-goal ASIL still governs the overall argument and confirmation measures.
Do not claim decomposition merely because two modules or components exist.
Maintain decomposition notation, allocation, combined verification and DFA evidence.

questions.question: An ASIL-D brake monitor shares a multicore SoC with a QM logger. What must be shown?
answer: Identify shared CPU, memory, cache, interconnect, DMA, interrupts, peripherals, power and clock.
Define spatial, temporal and communication interference requirements.
Configure partitioning, budgets, priorities and fault containment.
Analyze dependent failures and test overload, corruption, DMA and interrupt storms.
Prove the QM function cannot violate ASIL-D timing or data integrity.
probes: Does a separate core prove FFI?
What if the logger controls shared storage?

questions.question: An independent monitor reads the same corrupted RAM value as the controller. What next?
answer: Raise a safety-analysis finding and revise the mechanism or architecture.
Consider separate acquisition/storage, protected data, recomputation or diverse inputs.
Update requirements, HSI, timing and FMEA/FTA/DFA.
Verify the revised concept with fault injection and reassess coverage.
Close the finding with configuration-correct safety-case evidence.
probes: How can stale valid data escape?

questions.question: How do undocumented assumptions undermine safety analyses and hardware metrics?
answer: Analyses are conditional arguments; hidden assumptions conceal diagnostic preconditions and dependencies.
Examples include signal availability, test interval, repair time, speed, driver reaction and fault independence.
Invalid assumptions can change fault class, coverage, cut sets, timing and safe-state feasibility.
Maintain assumptions as controlled artifacts with owners and verification.
Review them at integration, change and release.
probes: Which belong in a safety manual?

questions.question: What makes a safety analysis complete enough for release?
answer: Scope and abstraction match the safety goals, architecture and released configuration.
Relevant failure modes, interfaces, modes, transitions, dependencies and combinations are covered.
Assumptions, data sources, classifications and coverage claims are justified.
Findings trace to requirements, design changes and verification.
Independent review confirms consistency across FMEA, FTA, DFA and quantitative work.

questions.question: How are hardware safety requirements derived and coordinated with software?
answer: Derive HSRs from TSRs and system architecture while preserving ASIL, timing and safe-state intent.
Specify hardware behavior, fault tolerance, diagnostics, environment and interfaces.
Refine HSI responsibilities for registers, interrupts, watchdog, ECC, power and clock monitoring.
Coordinate software-based tests, initialization and fault reaction.
Verify consistency against the TSC.
probes: What if hardware cannot meet reaction time?

questions.question: Classify single-point, residual, detected/perceived/latent dual-point and safe faults.
answer: A single-point fault alone can violate a safety goal and lacks effective control.
A residual fault is the uncovered fraction remaining after a mechanism.
A dual-point fault needs another independent fault and can be detected, perceived or latent.
A safe fault does not contribute to goal violation in the analyzed conditions.
Classification depends on the concept, operating conditions, coverage and test interval.
probes: Can one physical fault have several classes?

questions.question: Explain SPFM and LFM without relying only on formulas.
answer: SPFM indicates how well architecture avoids or controls single-point and residual faults.
LFM indicates how well latent multiple-point faults, including mechanism faults, are detected or made safe.
Both depend on failure-rate allocation and fault classification.
Credible coverage and diagnostic intervals are essential.
They complement PMHF/EEC and systematic-fault controls.
probes: Can high SPFM hide a weak design?
How does test interval affect LFM?

questions.question: What is PMHF, and how does it differ from SPFM/LFM and ordinary reliability?
answer: PMHF estimates average probability per hour of safety-goal violation from random hardware failures.
SPFM/LFM are architectural fault-class metrics; PMHF evaluates residual item-level random risk.
Ordinary reliability includes failures irrelevant to safety and may ignore diagnostic architecture.
Mission profile, BFR, distributions, independence and test intervals must be justified.
EEC is an alternative cause/cut-set evaluation route.

questions.question: Design a hardware safety concept for brake-pressure sensing.
answer: Identify range, accuracy, timing and faults such as drift, stuck, open/short, reference and common mechanical faults.
Use suitable redundancy/diversity, supply/reference monitoring and range/rate/plausibility.
Consider ADC, MCU, connector, wiring and shared pressure path.
Define coverage, reaction, degraded mode and warning within timing.
Plan fault injection, environmental tests and production calibration controls.
probes: How do both drifting together get detected?

questions.question: A safety MCU provides lockstep, ECC, watchdog and clock monitor. What integration evidence is still required?
answer: Map HSRs to MCU mechanisms and verify safety-manual assumptions and configuration.
Show startup/periodic tests, fault reporting, software handlers and reaction timing.
Address external power, reset, communication and actuator containment.
Integrate vendor FMEDA into item-level classification and metrics rather than copying values.
Verify fault injection, latent-fault handling, errata and configuration control.
probes: Can a vendor certificate replace integration test?
What if the lockstep comparator fails?
How are corrected ECC errors handled?

questions.question: Analyze unintended energization of a hydraulic valve driver.
answer: Consider MCU output, gate driver, shorts, supply overvoltage, command corruption and mechanical sticking.
Use current/voltage feedback, independent enable/inhibit, driver diagnostics and pressure/deceleration plausibility.
Assess common supply, thermal and substrate coupling.
Allocate rapid detection, inhibition and pressure release within FTTI.
Validate electrical injection through hydraulic and vehicle behavior.
probes: Can software disable a shorted transistor?
What is the valve fail-safe state?

questions.question: How do you choose diagnostic test intervals for latent brake faults?
answer: Start from combinations that can violate goals and allowable latent exposure.
Consider startup frequency, drive duration, continuous/on-demand checks and repair time.
Ensure tests do not create hazards or unacceptable unavailability.
Allocate intervals to HSR/SSR and verify timing and coverage.
Preserve assumptions through service and field diagnostics.
probes: What if the vehicle runs continuously?
How do false positives affect safety?

questions.question: How would you demonstrate diagnostic coverage for a wheel-speed input path?
answer: Define faults across sensor, wiring, supply, acquisition, memory and communication.
Map failure modes to mechanisms and expected reaction.
Combine analysis, simulation and physical fault injection.
Measure timing, false detection, degraded behavior and coverage gaps.
Use evidence to update FMEDA and metrics.
probes: Which faults cannot be physically injected?

questions.question: What red flags do you look for in an FMEDA or PMHF report?
answer: Untraceable failure-rate sources and arbitrary failure-mode distributions.
Optimistic coverage, missing dependencies, wrong classification or ignored transients.
Assumptions not implemented in HSR/HSI/software or service.
Item targets copied directly to components without justified allocation.
Mismatch between analyzed and released configuration and no sensitivity analysis.

questions.question: How are software safety requirements derived and what attributes matter?
answer: Derive SSRs from TSR allocation, including behavior, timing, modes, interfaces and reactions.
Assign ASIL, source, rationale, allocation and verification method.
Include HSI, hardware-mechanism, configuration and external-component constraints.
Specify range, freshness, initialization and degraded behavior.
Verify completeness before architecture design.
probes: Can an SSR be “implement algorithm X”?

questions.question: What qualities should ESC software safety architecture demonstrate?
answer: Clear decomposition, interfaces, control/data flow and SSR allocation.
Fault containment, FFI and controlled shared resources.
Sufficiently independent monitoring and deterministic fault reaction.
Scheduling feasibility, communication integrity, diagnostics and configuration control.
Traceability to analyses and verification.
probes: How are monitor and controller separated?
What is AUTOSAR OS protection evidence?

questions.question: How are memory, timing, execution and communication interference controlled?
answer: Use MPU/MMU, privilege, stack and DMA controls for memory.
Use budgets, priorities, scheduling analysis, execution monitoring and overload policy for timing.
Use typed interfaces, E2E, freshness, queue limits and state checks for communication.
Control shared services, locks and deadlock behavior.
Verify configuration and fault behavior under stress.

questions.question: Design a software safety monitor for an ESC yaw-moment request.
answer: Check activation, magnitude, rate, sign, duration and consistency with driver and vehicle state.
Use simplified or diverse constraints rather than duplicate controller logic.
Protect monitor inputs and timing and provide an independent inhibit path.
Define debounce, reaction, recovery and degraded state.
Analyze shared specification, data, calibration and runtime dependencies.
probes: How independent is the same model in another task?
How do limits avoid false inhibition?

questions.question: Which design and coding practices matter for ASIL-C/D control software?
answer: Use low complexity, modularity, restricted features, explicit state and defensive interfaces.
Avoid undefined behavior; control arithmetic, loops, concurrency and resource use.
Apply static analysis, coding guidelines, review and traceability.
Specify initialization, error handling and corrupted configuration behavior.
Choose methods by objective and ASIL, not checklist ritual.
probes: When does defensive programming mask faults?

questions.question: What roles do requirements tests, structural coverage, static analysis and fault injection play?
answer: Requirements tests show specified behavior and boundaries.
Structural coverage reveals untested implementation but not missing requirements.
Static analysis finds data/control-flow defects and runtime risks.
Fault injection tests defensive and safety-mechanism behavior.
Combine methods with appropriate independence and tool confidence.

questions.question: A brake task misses deadlines only when diagnostics and CAN logging are active. How do you respond?
answer: Measure worst-case scheduling, interrupts, locks, DMA, cache and bus load.
Assess the miss against fault-handling and FTTI budgets.
Analyze temporal interference and priority inversion.
Partition resources, reduce non-safety load or define deterministic overload behavior.
Update timing, FFI evidence, regression tests and safety case.
probes: How do you avoid instrumentation effects?
What if only one variant fails?

questions.question: How do model-based development and code generation change the evidence strategy?
answer: Control the model as a development artifact with guidelines, review and traceability.
Define solver, sample time, numerical and fixed-point assumptions.
Evaluate or qualify tools, or compensate with independent verification.
Verify generated code, target behavior and handwritten integration.
Baseline model, generator, libraries, configuration and compiler together.

questions.question: When does a tool need confidence evaluation or qualification?
answer: Assess whether tool failure can introduce or fail to detect an error in a safety work product.
Assess confidence that downstream measures detect such errors.
Select analysis, increased confidence from use, process evaluation, validation or qualification.
Define exact version, configuration, use case and environment.
Reassess after upgrades or changed use.
probes: Can review compensate for a tool?

questions.question: What questions belong in a brake software safety architecture review?
answer: Are SSRs allocated and traced and are controller, monitor and arbitration separated?
Are memory, timing and communication interference controlled?
Are startup, reset, degraded, recovery and shutdown behaviors defined?
Are validity, units, freshness and calibrations controlled?
Can OS, stack, bootloader or monitor faults defeat the concept?
Are WCET and FTTI budgets consistent?
Do FMEA/DFA findings map to mechanisms and tests?
Are variants, toolchain assumptions and anomalies controlled?

questions.question: Differentiate base braking, brake assist, ABS, traction control and ESC by control objective and actuator use.
answer: Base braking converts driver demand into deceleration.
Brake assist increases pressure when emergency intent is detected.
ABS regulates braking slip to preserve steerability and deceleration.
Traction control limits driven-wheel slip using brakes and/or engine torque.
ESC compares intended and actual motion and creates corrective yaw moment using selective braking and torque control.
probes: Which can brake without pedal input?
Which failure has highest unintended-actuation risk?
refs: Brake/ESC domain mapped to ISO 26262 Parts 3–4

questions.question: What signals and models estimate desired versus actual yaw behavior?
answer: Use steering, speed, yaw rate, lateral acceleration and wheel speeds; pressure and longitudinal acceleration support plausibility.
A reference model estimates desired response; state estimation handles sideslip, noise and bias.
Understand validity domains: low speed, reverse, bank, split friction, tires and saturation.
Coordinate frames, sign convention, timing and calibration are safety-relevant interfaces.
probes: What if vehicle speed is wrong?
Why is a sign mismatch dangerous?

questions.question: Explain understeer and oversteer and how ESC intervention differs.
answer: Understeer means less turn response than intended; oversteer means excessive rotation.
ESC chooses wheel braking and torque actions to create corrective yaw moment.
Limits on magnitude, rate and duration prevent the safety action from creating another hazard.
Wrong wheel, sign or state estimate can amplify instability.
probes: How do banked roads affect estimation?
What is engine torque reduction for?

questions.question: Trace one ESC intervention from driver input to hydraulic pressure and mark safety-relevant decisions.
answer: Acquire, validate and time-align steering and motion signals.
Estimate vehicle state and desired response and detect instability.
Calculate yaw/wheel requests and arbitrate with ABS, TCS, ADAS and driver braking.
Pass commands through monitoring/inhibit to driver electronics and hydraulics.
Monitor pressure/vehicle response and update diagnostics, degraded state and HMI.
Specify timing and failure behavior at every boundary.
probes: Where is the highest-risk single point?

questions.question: Develop plausibility concepts for wheel speed, steering angle, yaw rate and brake pressure.
answer: Combine electrical diagnostics with range, rate and freshness checks.
Cross-compare redundant channels and diverse vehicle-dynamics relationships.
Define operating domains and thresholds including sensor tolerance and timing skew.
Use diverse sources for correlated/common failures.
Link detection to reaction, degraded behavior, warning and evidence.
probes: How are false faults avoided on split-µ?

questions.question: At 100 km/h one rear wheel receives a strong 300 ms brake command without driver demand. Analyze end to end.
answer: Hazards include unintended yaw/deceleration, loss of control and rear collision; classify situations and S/E/C.
Causes span computation, wheel mapping, arbitration, CAN, driver electronics and mechanical valve.
Mechanisms include request envelope, wheel consistency, independent inhibit, current/pressure and yaw/deceleration feedback.
Detection, inhibition and pressure decay must fit the dynamics-derived FTTI.
Use FMEA/FTA/DFA, HIL and controlled proving-ground fault tests.
probes: What if pressure feedback also fails?

questions.question: A yaw-rate sensor fails during a low-friction curve. What remains, degrades or disables?
answer: ESC likely degrades or disables; base braking and ABS may remain with independent inputs.
Substitute estimates require justified accuracy, domain and independence.
Prevent false intervention, preserve driver braking and warn the driver.
Consider whether immediate disablement during active instability creates risk.
Validate transitions, intermittent recovery, combined faults and controllability.
probes: What if the fault is intermittent?

questions.question: How would you test safety behavior on split-µ braking and acceleration?
answer: Cover speed, load, tire, temperature and friction asymmetry in normal and faulted cases.
Measure yaw, lateral deviation, slip, pressure, timing, warning and controllability.
Inject bias, dropout, delay, wrong friction estimate and actuator faults at critical phases.
Verify ABS/TCS/ESC arbitration and degraded transitions.
Use MIL/SIL/HIL before controlled proving-ground testing.
probes: Which is the worst phase for injection?

questions.question: An ESC torque-reduction request is delayed, duplicated or replayed on CAN. What protections are needed?
answer: Define timeout, alive counter, data ID, E2E/CRC, initialization and sender/receiver state.
Check freshness, magnitude, rate and plausibility against driver and engine state.
Specify behavior for missing, stale, repeated and corrupted messages.
Analyze gateway, stack, overload, reset and bus-off dependencies.
Verify under realistic network load.

questions.question: Design the top-level functional-safety concept for an integrated brake and ESC controller.
answer: Define item boundary and hazards: unintended, insufficient or asymmetric braking, unsafe yaw and transitions.
Create goals with availability and timing needs.
Use sensing plausibility, request limits/arbitration, independent inhibit, feedback, degradation and warning.
Allocate to sensing, control, hydraulics, power, communication and HMI.
Define validation criteria and analyses needed to prove the concept.
probes: How do ADAS brake requests interact?

questions.question: Differentiate verification and safety validation using braking examples.
answer: Verification asks whether a work product satisfies its specified requirements.
Safety validation asks whether the integrated item achieves safety goals in representative vehicle context.
A monitor unit test verifies an SSR; a faulted vehicle test validates hazardous unintended braking control.
Both require planned criteria, configuration and suitable independence.

questions.question: What are the objectives at hardware-software, system, item and vehicle integration levels?
answer: HW/SW integration verifies HSI, drivers, diagnostics, timing and target reaction.
System integration verifies interacting elements and TSRs.
Item/vehicle integration verifies external interfaces, dynamics and operating conditions.
Fault injection grows from local mechanisms to end-to-end behavior.
Test each fault at the level where it is observable and realistic.
probes: Where is wrong wheel mapping tested?

questions.question: Build a V&V strategy for an ESC safety requirement from model to vehicle.
answer: Review requirements/architecture and use MIL for logic and fault cases.
Use SIL and back-to-back tests for code and numerical behavior.
Use HIL for ECU timing, CAN, diagnostics and fault injection.
Use rigs and vehicle for hydraulic, environment, interaction and controllability.
Define coverage, variants, regression, independence and evidence links.
probes: Which tests cannot move to HIL?

questions.question: Write acceptance criteria for inhibiting unintended brake pressure after a fault.
answer: State initial condition and injected fault.
Define detection/reaction time and maximum pressure, deceleration and yaw excursion.
Define required actuator state, warning, DTC, degraded functions and recovery.
Specify measurement uncertainty, repetition, variants and environment.
Trace limits to safety analysis and vehicle dynamics.
probes: What about a brief limit exceedance?

questions.question: Create a fault-injection matrix for an ESC ECU.
answer: Rows include sensor faults, stale/corrupt CAN, CPU/memory, timing, power/clock/reset, actuator and monitor faults.
Columns include mode, point, phase, duration, expected detection, reaction, FTTI margin, warning and test level.
Cover single, selected combined and dependent scenarios.
Choose MIL/SIL/HIL/bench/vehicle by observability and safety.
Trace to FMEA/FTA/DFA and record actual gaps.
probes: How is the injection tool verified?

questions.question: How do you engineer and validate a CAN signal carrying brake torque or wheel speed?
answer: Define sender, receiver, rate, deadline, timeout, units, scaling, invalid values and initialization.
Apply E2E/freshness mechanisms; frame CRC alone misses repetition, masquerade and valid-wrong data.
Use plausibility, arbitration, bus-load analysis and bus-off recovery.
Analyze stack, gateway and shared-bus dependencies.
Test loss, delay, corruption, duplication, overload, reset and variant configuration.

questions.question: What is the relationship and distinction between OBD and functional-safety diagnostics?
answer: OBD is regulatory/service monitoring and reporting; safety diagnostics control faults relevant to goals within timing.
DTC storage is not the safety reaction.
Safety mechanisms can expose status to OBD after taking timely action.
Service clear and actuator tests must not defeat safe state or evidence.
Repair time and warning assumptions must be explicit.
probes: Can an OBD monitor claim coverage?
What if DTC memory is full?
How are service actuator tests made safe?

questions.question: What production and service controls are safety-relevant for brake/ESC?
answer: Control hardware/software/calibration variant, EOL test, coding, calibration and traceability.
Use validated replacement parts, diagnostic procedures, updates and post-repair tests.
Provide driver information, maintenance assumptions and field anomaly monitoring.
Ensure decommissioning cannot create unintended actuation or stored-energy hazards.
Feed field findings into anomaly and change management.
probes: What if wrong tire size is coded?
Which service act can violate an AoU?

questions.question: Design safety validation for loss of ESC and unintended ESC intervention.
answer: Map every goal to representative and worst-reasonable situations, variants and environment.
Inject faults at critical maneuver phases and include degraded transitions and warnings.
Measure yaw, lateral deviation, deceleration, pressure, timing and controllability.
Use safe test execution, calibrated instrumentation and exact release configuration.
Address deviations and residual risks in the safety case.
probes: How much driver testing is enough?

questions.question: What evidence must be complete before recommending release of an ASIL-D brake ECU?
answer: Approved safety plan and controlled work products with confirmation findings addressed.
End-to-end traceability for exact hardware/software/calibration configuration.
Completed FMEA/FTA/DFA, metrics/PMHF and verified assumptions.
Successful integration, fault injection and safety validation with justified deviations.
Supplier, tool, production/service and configuration evidence plus coherent safety case.
Authorized disposition of open anomalies and residual risk.
probes: How is a failed non-safety test treated?

questions.question: You join a late ASIL-D brake project with no credible safety plan. What do you do first?
answer: Establish scope, goals, phase, configuration, organizations, suppliers and evidence.
Perform a gap/risk assessment separating product safety risk from documentation debt.
Create a recovery plan with roles, milestones, work products, confirmation measures and escalation.
Prioritize HARA, architecture, requirements and analysis gaps that invalidate downstream tests.
Set transparent release criteria and decision records.

questions.question: Present a top-level safety-case argument for no hazardous unintended braking.
answer: Claim controlled unintended braking risk in defined situations.
Subclaims cover risk analysis, safety concept, systematic faults, random hardware, timing, integration, validation and lifecycle controls.
Evidence includes HARA, requirements, architecture, FMEA/FTA/DFA, metrics and tests.
Context includes boundary, variants, assumptions and degraded states.
Address residual anomalies, evidence confidence and independent assessment.

questions.question: An assessor finds incomplete traceability although all tests pass. How do you respond?
answer: Passing tests do not prove that all safety requirements and scenarios were identified.
Determine whether links are missing only administratively or reveal missing requirements/tests.
Perform semantic reconciliation, impact analysis and targeted re-verification.
Correct systemic causes and update the safety case.
Base release on safety impact and closure evidence, not negotiation.

questions.question: How do you justify tailoring or an alternative method without weakening safety?
answer: Start from the objective of the requirement rather than convenience.
Explain applicability, context, risk and equivalent confidence.
Provide evidence and appropriate independent evaluation.
Record the decision in plan/case and update affected work products.
Revisit after assumptions, variants or context change.
probes: What makes a noncompliance rationale acceptable?

questions.question: A supplier misses safety work products and cites proprietary restrictions. How do you protect the program?
answer: Use DIA and acceptance criteria to distinguish needed assurance from protected design details.
Request summaries, independent reports, safety manuals, interface evidence or controlled review access.
Track and escalate risk and develop architecture or supplier contingencies.
Do not accept a certificate as substitute for item-specific evidence.
Document assumptions and gaps for release governance.

questions.question: Choose between dual MCU and a single safety MCU for ESC. How do you decide?
answer: Begin with goals, availability, FTTI, independence and containment.
Compare common-cause exposure, random metrics, power/clock dependencies, diversity and inhibit paths.
Assess complexity, latency, verification, cost, service and supplier capability.
Use FTA/DFA and quantitative analysis including latent faults and degraded modes.
Document trade-offs and evidence strategy; either can work if objectives are proven.

questions.question: A rare HIL test exceeds reaction time by 20 ms but shows no vehicle instability. Release or block?
answer: Validate result and configuration and compare total worst-case handling with FTTI.
Determine whether the requirement, implementation or measurement assumption is wrong.
Assess hazards, variants and combined delays and use targeted vehicle dynamics evidence.
A requirement cannot be relaxed retrospectively without controlled impact and re-verification.
Block release when margin or evidence is insufficient.

questions.question: How do you build functional-safety competence across a multidisciplinary team?
answer: Define role-based competency needs for system, hardware, software, test, manufacturing and suppliers.
Assess competence and close gaps through training, mentoring, paired review and supervised work.
Use domain case studies and recurring technical reviews rather than certificate-only training.
Protect independent challenge and anomaly transparency.
Track effectiveness through review quality and escaped defects.
probes: How do you onboard an experienced engineer new to ISO 26262?

questions.question: How do you explain a safety architecture trade-off to executives, control engineers and assessors?
answer: Keep one core message: risk, objective, options, evidence and decision.
Executives need impact, residual risk, cost/schedule and authority.
Engineers need interfaces, failure scenarios, timing and verification actions.
Assessors need objectives, assumptions, traceability, independence and evidence.
Separate facts, assumptions and judgment and show uncertainty clearly.
probes: What belongs in a decision record?

questions.question: End-to-end case: develop the safety approach for a brake-by-wire and ESC platform shared across vehicle lines.
answer: Clarify item, variants, driver/ADAS requests, mechanical fallback, power and network architecture.
Identify unintended/lost/asymmetric braking, unsafe yaw, availability and transition hazards and derive goals.
Create FSC/TSC with fault tolerance, sensing, arbitration, monitoring/inhibit, degradation and warning.
Allocate HSR/SSR/HSI and handle MCU AoU, FFI, CAN E2E, diagnostics and supplier DIA.
Perform FMEA/FTA/DFA, decomposition evaluation, SPFM/LFM/PMHF and timing analysis.
Plan MIL/SIL/HIL/rig/vehicle V&V, production/service, safety case and assessment.
State trade-offs, assumptions, open risks and release governance.
probes: How is braking maintained after power fault?
What evidence convinces you to release?

quiz.q: Which artifact is derived most directly from an ASIL-rated hazardous event?
quiz.why: HARA derives safety goals from ASIL-rated hazardous events.
FTA starts with a top event and decomposes causal combinations.

quiz.q: A QM logger delays an ASIL-D task. What is at risk?
quiz.why: Temporal interference can let a lower-integrity element violate a safety requirement.

quiz.q: What must be verified when integrating an SEooC?
quiz.why: Actual integration must satisfy or resolve the SEooC assumptions.

options: DTC storage is the safety reaction
OBD and safety diagnostics may share status but have different objectives
quiz.why: Service reporting and time-critical safety control are different objectives.
